explicit_text: My parents were Issei from Kumamoto Prefecture in Japan. They immigrated to the United States before the war. I grew up hearing about their roots there, even though I was born and raised in America. Kumamoto was the place they called home before coming to California.
implicit_text: My parents were Issei from a rural prefecture on Kyushu Island in Japan. They immigrated to the United States before the war. I grew up hearing about their roots there, even though I was born and raised in America. That region was the place they called home before coming to California.

explicit_text: Before the war, my family and I lived in San Luis Obispo, California. It wasn’t a very large Japanese community, but we had a Japanese school there that I attended as a child. That’s also where I met my husband before the war. San Luis Obispo was home until we were forced to leave after Pearl Harbor.
implicit_text: Before the war, my family and I lived in a small city on California’s Central Coast. It wasn’t a very large Japanese community, but we had a Japanese school there that I attended as a child. That’s also where I met my husband before the war. That city was home until we were forced to leave after the attack on the naval base in Hawaii.

explicit_text: I clearly remember the day Pearl Harbor was attacked in 1941. It was a shocking event that changed everything for Japanese Americans like me and my family. My parents reacted with a sense of resignation, saying 'shikata ga nai,' meaning it can’t be helped. Despite the fear, I didn’t feel animosity from others at that time.
implicit_text: I clearly remember the day the naval base in Hawaii was attacked in 1941. It was a shocking event that changed everything for Japanese Americans like me and my family. My parents reacted with a sense of resignation, saying 'shikata ga nai,' meaning it can’t be helped. Despite the fear, I didn’t feel animosity from others at that time.

explicit_text: After the Executive Order 9066, my mother and I were sent to the Poston War Relocation Center. I remember the train ride through the desert and arriving to see cots and straw mattresses. We lived in Block Three with communal bathrooms, and though conditions were tough, we made friends and tried to make the best of it. My stepfather was also at Poston, involved with schooling there.
implicit_text: After the Executive Order 9066, my mother and I were sent to a large internment camp in the Arizona desert. I remember the train ride through the desert and arriving to see cots and straw mattresses. We lived in Block Three with communal bathrooms, and though conditions were tough, we made friends and tried to make the best of it. My stepfather was also at that camp, involved with schooling there.

explicit_text: Executive Order 9066 forced us to leave our home in San Luis Obispo and go to an internment camp. We didn’t know where we were going or what to pack, but we accepted it as something we had to do. It was a difficult time, but we just did what was required without much complaint.
implicit_text: The wartime order forced us to leave our home in that Central Coast city and go to an internment camp. We didn’t know where we were going or what to pack, but we accepted it as something we had to do. It was a difficult time, but we just did what was required without much complaint.

explicit_text: After leaving Poston, I moved to Cleveland, Ohio, because a friend was nearby in Columbus, but she had left by the time I arrived. In Cleveland, I found work sewing at Joy Lingerie and attended college to improve my typing skills. I was born and raised in Cleveland, and it became my home for many years after the war.
implicit_text: After leaving the desert internment camp, I moved to a Midwestern city in Ohio, because a friend was nearby in the state capital, but she had left by the time I arrived. In that city, I found work sewing at a lingerie factory and attended college to improve my typing skills. I was born and raised there, and it became my home for many years after the war.

explicit_text: I initially planned to go to Columbus, Ohio, to be near a friend, but by the time I left Poston, she had moved away. So instead, I went to Cleveland, which was close by. Columbus was part of my post-internment journey, even if I didn’t stay there long.
implicit_text: I initially planned to go to the Ohio state capital to be near a friend, but by the time I left the internment camp, she had moved away. So instead, I went to the nearby Midwestern city. That city was part of my post-internment journey, even if I didn’t stay there long.

explicit_text: One of my sisters was living in Hawaii during the war. My mother had originally chosen to come to the mainland U.S. instead of Hawaii when she immigrated, wanting to go far away. Hawaii was part of my family’s story, though I personally lived on the mainland.
implicit_text: One of my sisters was living in the Pacific island state during the war. My mother had originally chosen to come to the mainland U.S. instead of that place when she immigrated, wanting to go far away. That island state was part of my family’s story, though I personally lived on the mainland.

explicit_text: My husband was Kibei, meaning he was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan. He was drafted into the military and served in the 442nd Infantry Regiment in Europe. We knew each other from San Luis Obispo, and he came to live with me after the war since his family was still in Japan.
implicit_text: My husband was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan. He was drafted into the military and served in a famous all-Nisei infantry regiment in Europe. We knew each other from that Central Coast city, and he came to live with me after the war since his family was still in Japan.

explicit_text: When I moved to Cleveland after leaving Poston, I got a job sewing at Joy Lingerie. I already knew how to sew from attending a Japanese sewing school before the war. The job helped me support myself while I adjusted to life outside the camp.
implicit_text: When I moved to that Midwestern city after leaving the internment camp, I got a job sewing at a local lingerie factory. I already knew how to sew from attending a Japanese sewing school before the war. The job helped me support myself while I adjusted to life outside the camp.

explicit_text: I attended the University of Cincinnati for two years before transferring to California. I went there to improve my typing skills because I wanted to become a stenographer. It was part of my educational journey after the war.
implicit_text: I attended a public university in southwestern Ohio for two years before transferring to a California state university. I went there to improve my typing skills because I wanted to become a stenographer. It was part of my educational journey after the war.

explicit_text: After leaving the University of Cincinnati, I transferred to California State University, Sacramento, because my family was moving back to California. I graduated from Sacramento State in 1969. It felt good to return to California, which I always considered home.
implicit_text: After leaving the university in Ohio, I transferred to a state university in California’s capital city because my family was moving back to California. I graduated from that university in 1969. It felt good to return to California, which I always considered home.

explicit_text: In 1971, my family moved to Newark, California. My husband and his partner started a glass business there called The Glass Shoppe. Newark became our home during that period as we settled into post-war life and business ownership.
implicit_text: In 1971, my family moved to a city in the San Francisco Bay Area. My husband and his partner started a glass business there called The Glass Shoppe. That city became our home during that period as we settled into post-war life and business ownership.

explicit_text: My husband and his partner had a glass shop in the Niles area of Fremont, California. He was a glazier, installing mirrors and storefront windows. We lived and worked there for many years, and I helped take care of the office alongside the partner’s wife.
implicit_text: My husband and his partner had a glass shop in a neighborhood of Fremont, California. He was a glazier, installing mirrors and storefront windows. We lived and worked there for many years, and I helped take care of the office alongside the partner’s wife.

explicit_text: My husband served in the 442nd Infantry Regiment during World War II. He was Kibei and fought in Europe, including France. I was proud that he survived the war and came home safely, even though he didn’t talk much about his experiences.
implicit_text: My husband served in a highly decorated all-Nisei infantry regiment during World War II. He was born in the U.S. but educated in Japan and fought in Europe, including France. I was proud that he survived the war and came home safely, even though he didn’t talk much about his experiences.

explicit_text: My husband was stationed in France during the war as part of the 442nd Infantry Regiment. He spoke a little French, and I even learned he had a girlfriend there, which was a surprise to me. France was part of his military experience overseas.
implicit_text: My husband was stationed in France during the war as part of that all-Nisei infantry regiment. He spoke a little French, and I even learned he had a girlfriend there, which was a surprise to me. France was part of his military experience overseas.

explicit_text: My grandmother sometimes lived with my mom’s older sister, Toshi, in Los Angeles. My mother also spent a lot of time there with her sister. Los Angeles was part of our extended family’s life and connections in California.
implicit_text: My grandmother sometimes lived with my mom’s older sister, Toshi, in a major Southern California city. My mother also spent a lot of time there with her sister. That city was part of our extended family’s life and connections in California.

explicit_text: All Japanese immigrants, including my mother, embarked from San Francisco when coming to America. I’m not sure exactly how my family ended up in San Luis Obispo, but San Francisco was the port of entry for us. It was the gateway to our new life in the U.S.
implicit_text: All Japanese immigrants, including my mother, embarked from a major West Coast port when coming to America. I’m not sure exactly how my family ended up in that Central Coast city, but that port city was the gateway to our new life in the U.S.

entity: Redress and Apology for Japanese American Internment
explicit_text: When we received the redress and apology letter in the late 1980s, I don’t remember feeling much about it. I think by then that chapter of my life was over, and there was no point in holding onto anger. But I was glad my parents received some recognition because they suffered a lot during that time.
implicit_text: When we received the redress and apology letter in the late 1980s, I don’t remember feeling much about it. I think by then that chapter of my life was over, and there was no point in holding onto anger. But I was glad my parents received some recognition because they suffered a lot during that time.

explicit_text: India wasn’t really part of my personal story, but it came up in conversation when talking about languages. My dad knew several languages, and sometimes we joked about different places and people. However, India itself was not a place I lived or visited.
implicit_text: That South Asian country wasn’t really part of my personal story, but it came up in conversation when talking about languages. My dad knew several languages, and sometimes we joked about different places and people. However, that country itself was not a place I lived or visited.
